questions.question: What does the leading underscore on directories like `_layouts/`, `_includes/`, and `_posts/` signal to Jekyll?
choices: They are hidden from the file system
They contain private content that should never be deployed
They are special directories that Jekyll recognises by name and handles with specific rules
They are reserved for plugins only
questions.explanation: The underscore prefix marks special directories. Jekyll knows `_posts`, `_layouts`, `_includes`, `_data`, `_drafts`, and `_site` by name and applies specific behaviour to each; regular directories are just copied to the build output.

questions.question: You change `title` in `_config.yml` while `bundle exec jekyll serve` is running, but the browser still shows the old title even after a hard reload. What's going on?
choices: The browser cache is stuck; clear it and try again
Jekyll reads `_config.yml` once at startup; the file watcher does not reload it
`_config.yml` only takes effect when deployed to GitHub Pages
The `title` key was deprecated in recent Jekyll versions
questions.explanation: Jekyll loads `_config.yml` at the start of a build. The watcher rebuilds on content/template changes but does not pick up config changes - you must stop and restart `jekyll serve`.

questions.question: Should `_site/` be committed to your blog's Git repository?
choices: Yes - it is the production output and deploys depend on it
Yes - committing it lets reviewers see rendered HTML in diffs
No - it is build output that Jekyll wipes and rewrites on every build; treat it like a `dist/` directory
No - but only because it contains secrets
questions.explanation: `_site/` is pure build output, regenerated from source on every build. Committing it bloats history and causes merge conflicts. Add it to `.gitignore` and commit the source files instead.
